    ]
  },
  {
    "code_dossier": {
      "code_purpose": "util",
      "description": "Environment variable utility for boolean value parsing",
      "file_path": "src/kimi_cli/utils/envvar.py",
      "functions": [
        "get_env_bool"
      ],
      "importance_score": 0.6,
      "interfaces": [
        "get_env_bool"
      ],
      "name": "envvar.py",
      "source_summary": "from __future__ import annotations\n\nimport os\n\n_TRUE_VALUES = {\"1\", \"true\", \"t\", \"yes\", \"y\"}\n\n\ndef get_env_bool(name: str, default: bool = False) -> bool:\n    value = os.getenv(name)\n    if value is None:\n        return default\n    return value.strip().lower() in _TRUE_VALUES\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 2.0,
      "lines_of_code": 12,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 1

        "name": "__future__",
        "path": null,
        "version": null
      }
    ],
    "detailed_description": "This component provides utility functions for parsing environment variables as boolean values. The get_env_bool function converts string environment variables to boolean values by checking against a predefined set of truthy string representations. It handles case-insensitive comparison and whitespace trimming, returning a default value when the environment variable is not set. This utility is essential for configuration management and command-line interface applications that need to parse boolean flags from environment variables.",

  {
    "code_dossier": {
      "code_purpose": "util",
      "description": null,
      "file_path": "src/kimi_cli/utils/media_tags.py",
      "functions": [
        "_format_tag",
        "wrap_media_part"
      ],
      "importance_score": 0.6,
      "interfaces": [
        "_format_tag",
        "wrap_media_part"
      ],
      "name": "media_tags.py",
      "source_summary": "from __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom collections.abc import Mapping\nfrom html import escape\n\nfrom kimi_cli.wire.types import ContentPart, TextPart\n\n\ndef _format_tag(tag: str, attrs: Mapping[str, str | None] | None = None) -> str:\n    if not attrs:\n        return f\"<{tag}>\"\n    rendered: list[str] = []\n    for key, value in sorted(attrs.items()):\n        if not value:\n            continue\n        rendered.append(f'{key}=\"{escape(str(value), quote=True)}\"')\n    if not rendered:\n        return f\"<{tag}>\"\n    return f\"<{tag} \" + \" \".join(rendered) + \">\"\n\n\ndef wrap_media_part(\n    part: ContentPart, *, tag: str, attrs: Mapping[str, str | None] | None = None\n) -> list[ContentPart]:\n    return [\n        TextPart(text=_format_tag(tag, attrs)),\n        part,\n        TextPart(text=f\"</{tag}>\"),\n    ]\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 5.0,
      "lines_of_code": 29,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 2
    },
    "dependencies": [
      {
        "dependency_type": "standard_library",
        "is_external": false,

      },

        "line_number": 6,

      }
    ],
    "detailed_description": "This component provides utility functions for generating HTML tags and wrapping media content parts with HTML tags. The _format_tag function creates properly formatted HTML opening tags with escaped attribute values, while wrap_media_part creates a sequence of content parts that represent an HTML-tagged media element with opening tag, content, and closing tag. This is particularly useful for safely embedding media content in HTML contexts while preventing XSS vulnerabilities through proper escaping.",

      ],
      "name": "__init__.py",
      "source_summary": "\"\"\"Project-wide Rich configuration helpers.\"\"\"\n\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nimport re\nfrom typing import Final\n\nfrom rich import _wrap\n\n# Regex used by Rich to compute break opportunities during wrapping.\n_DEFAULT_WRAP_PATTERN: Final[re.Pattern[str]] = re.compile(r\"\\s*\\S+\\s*\")\n_CHAR_WRAP_PATTERN: Final[re.Pattern[str]] = re.compile(r\".\", re.DOTALL)\n\n\ndef enable_character_wrap() -> None:\n    \"\"\"Switch Rich's wrapping logic to break on every character.\n\n    Rich's default behavior tries to preserve whole words; we override the\n    internal regex so markdown rendering can fold text at any column once it\n    exceeds the terminal width.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    _wrap.re_word = _CHAR_WRAP_PATTERN\n\n\ndef restore_word_wrap() -> None:\n    \"\"\"Restore Rich's default word-based wrapping.\"\"\"\n\n    _wrap.re_word = _DEFAULT_WRAP_PATTERN\n\n\n# Apply character-based wrapping globally for the CLI.\nenable_character_wrap()\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 2.0,
      "lines_of_code": 33,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 2
    },
    "dependencies": [
      {
        "dependency_type": "builtin",
        "is_external": false,

      },
      {
        "dependency_type": "builtin",
        "is_external": false,

      },
      {

      },

        "path": null,
        "version": null
      }
    ],
    "detailed_description": "This component provides utility functions to configure Rich's text wrapping behavior globally across the project. It overrides Rich's default word-based wrapping with character-based wrapping, allowing text to break at any character position when exceeding terminal width. This is particularly useful for markdown rendering in CLI applications where precise column control is needed. The module automatically applies character-based wrapping upon import.",
    "interfaces": [
      {
        "description": "Switch Rich's wrapping logic to break on every character",

  {
    "code_dossier": {
      "code_purpose": "util",
      "description": "String manipulation utilities for text shortening and random string generation",
      "file_path": "src/kimi_cli/utils/string.py",
      "functions": [
        "shorten_middle",
        "random_string"
      ],
      "importance_score": 0.6,
      "interfaces": [
        "shorten_middle",
        "random_string"
      ],
      "name": "string.py",
      "source_summary": "from __future__ import annotations\n\nimport random\nimport re\nimport string\n\n_NEWLINE_RE = re.compile(r\"[\\r\\n]+\")\n\n\ndef shorten_middle(text: str, width: int, remove_newline: bool = True) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Shorten the text by inserting ellipsis in the middle.\"\"\"\n    if len(text) <= width:\n        return text\n    if remove_newline:\n        text = _NEWLINE_RE.sub(\" \", text)\n    return text[: width // 2] + \"...\" + text[-width // 2 :]\n\n\ndef random_string(length: int = 8) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Generate a random string of fixed length.\"\"\"\n    letters = string.ascii_lowercase\n    return \"\".join(random.choice(letters) for _ in range(length))\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 4.0,
      "lines_of_code": 22,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 2
    },

      },

Both functions are designed as pure utility functions with clear input/output contracts.",

  {
    "code_dossier": {
      "code_purpose": "util",
      "description": "Utility functions for managing process and terminal titles in the Kimi CLI application",
      "file_path": "src/kimi_cli/utils/proctitle.py",
      "functions": [
        "set_process_title",
        "set_terminal_title",
        "init_process_name"
      ],
      "importance_score": 0.6,
      "interfaces": [
        "set_process_title",
        "set_terminal_title",
        "init_process_name"
      ],
      "name": "proctitle.py",
      "source_summary": "from __future__ import annotations\n\nimport sys\n\n\ndef set_process_title(title: str) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Set the OS-level process title visible in ps/top/terminal panels.\"\"\"\n    try:\n        import setproctitle\n\n        setproctitle.setproctitle(title)\n    except ImportError:\n        pass\n\n\ndef set_terminal_title(title: str) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Set the terminal tab/window title via ANSI OSC escape sequence.\n\n    Only writes when stderr is a TTY to avoid polluting piped output.\n    \"\"\"\n    if not sys.stderr.isatty():\n        return\n    try:\n        sys.stderr.write(f\"\\033]0;{title}\\007\")\n        sys.stderr.flush()\n    except OSError:\n        pass\n\n\ndef init_process_name(name: str = \"Kimi Code\") -> None:\n    \"\"\"Initialize process name: OS process title + terminal tab title.\"\"\"\n    set_process_title(name)\n    set_terminal_title(name)\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 2.0,
      "lines_of_code": 33,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 3
    },
    "dependencies": [
      {
        "dependency_type": "standard_library",
        "is_external": false,
        "line_number": 3,
        "name": "sys",
        "path": null,
        "version": null
      },

        "dependency_type": "third_party",
        "is_external": true,
        "line_number": 8,
        "name": "setproctitle",
        "path": null,
        "version": null
      },
      {
        "dependency_type": "standard_library",
        "is_external": false,
        "line_number": 1,
        "name": "__future__",
        "path": null,
        "version": null
      }
    ],
    "detailed_description": "This component provides utility functions for managing process visibility in the Kimi CLI application. It handles both OS-level process titles (visible in system monitoring tools like ps/top) and terminal tab/window titles via ANSI escape sequences. The component is designed with graceful degradation - it will silently fail if dependencies are missing or operations encounter errors, ensuring the core application functionality remains unaffected.",

  {
    "code_dossier": {
      "code_purpose": "util",
      "description": null,
      "file_path": "src/kimi_cli/utils/typing.py",
      "functions": [
        "flatten_union"
      ],
      "importance_score": 0.6,
      "interfaces": [
        "flatten_union"
      ],
      "name": "typing.py",
      "source_summary": "from types import UnionType\nfrom typing import Any, TypeAliasType, Union, get_args, get_origin\n\n\ndef flatten_union(tp: Any) -> tuple[Any, ...]:\n    \"\"\"\n    If `tp` is a `UnionType`, return its flattened arguments as a tuple.\n    Otherwise, return a tuple with `tp` as the only element.\n    \"\"\"\n    if isinstance(tp, TypeAliasType):\n        tp = tp.__value__\n    origin = get_origin(tp)\n    if origin in (UnionType, Union):\n        args = get_args(tp)\n        flattened_args: list[Any] = []\n        for arg in args:\n            flattened_args.extend(flatten_union(arg))\n        return tuple(flattened_args)\n    else:\n        return (tp,)\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 4.0,
      "lines_of_code": 20,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 1
    },

        "dependency_type": "standard_library",

        "version": null
      }
    ],

It handles TypeAliasType resolution and processes nested union structures through recursive calls, providing normalized type representations for type system operations.",
    "interfaces": [

          {

      "code_purpose": "util",
      "description": "Utility module providing share directory path resolution and initialization for the Kimi CLI application",
      "file_path": "src/kimi_cli/share.py",
      "functions": [
        "get_share_dir"
      ],
      "importance_score": 0.6,
      "interfaces": [
        "get_share_dir"
      ],
      "name": "share.py",
      "source_summary": "from __future__ import annotations\n\nimport os\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\n\ndef get_share_dir() -> Path:\n    \"\"\"Get the share directory path.\"\"\"\n    if share_dir := os.getenv(\"KIMI_SHARE_DIR\"):\n        share_dir = Path(share_dir)\n    else:\n        share_dir = Path.home() / \".kimi\"\n    share_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    return share_dir\n"
    },
    "complexity_metrics": {
      "cyclomatic_complexity": 2.0,
      "lines_of_code": 14,
      "number_of_classes": 0,
      "number_of_functions": 1
    },

        "path": null,
        "version": null
      }
    ],
    "detailed_description": "This utility module manages the application's shared resource directory location. The `get_share_dir()` function determines the appropriate storage location by checking the KIMI_SHARE_DIR environment variable first, then falling back to the default ~/.kimi directory in the user's home. It ensures the directory exists before returning the Path object, making it safe for immediate use by other components that need persistent storage or shared resources. The function uses modern Python syntax (walrus operator) and pathlib for cross-platform compatibility.\"",

      "description": "Prompt template loader module that initializes and exposes system prompt templates for the kimi_cli application. Reads markdown template files (init.md and compact.md) and provides them as module-level constants for use by other components in the system.",

      "functions": [],

      "name": "__init__.py",
      "source_summary": "from __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nINIT = (Path(__file__).parent / \"init.md\").read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\nCOMPACT = (Path(__file__).parent / \"compact.md\").read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\n"
    },

    "detailed_description": "This module serves as a prompt template loader and registry for the kimi_cli application. It loads two markdown-based prompt templates from the local filesystem at module import time: INIT (from init.md) and COMPACT (from compact.md). The module uses pathlib for cross-platform file path resolution and reads the template content with explicit UTF-8 encoding. The templates are exposed as module-level string constants, providing a centralized and convenient way for other components (likely CLI commands or AI agent modules) to access system prompts without dealing with file I/O operations. This design pattern centralizes prompt management and ensures consistent prompt content across the application.\"",

    "code_dossier": {
      "code_purpose": "entry",
      "description": "Entry point module for the multi-session ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) server that initializes logging and starts the asynchronous server on stdio transport",

      "functions": [
        "acp_main"

      "importance_score": 0.6,

        "acp_main"
      ],
      "name": "__init__.py",
      "source_summary": "def acp_main() -> None:\n    \"\"\"Entry point for the multi-session ACP server.\"\"\"\n    import asyncio\n\n    import acp\n\n    from kimi_cli.acp.server import ACPServer\n    from kimi_cli.app import enable_logging\n    from kimi_cli.utils.logging import logger\n\n    enable_logging()\n    logger.info(\"Starting ACP server on stdio\")\n    asyncio.run(acp.run_agent(ACPServer(), use_unstable_protocol=True))\n"
    },

    "detailed_description": "This module serves as the main entry point for the ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) multi-session server. The `acp_main()` function is responsible for orchestrating the server initialization and startup sequence. It performs the following operations in sequence:\n\n1. **Logging Initialization**: Calls `enable_logging()` to set up the logging infrastructure before any server operations begin\n2. **Server Startup Notification**: Logs an informational message indicating the ACP server is starting on stdio transport\n3. **Asynchronous Server Execution**: Creates an instance of `ACPServer` and runs it using the `acp.run_agent()` function with asyncio event loop management\n4. **Protocol Configuration**: Enables the unstable protocol version (`use_unstable_protocol=True`) for the ACP server, indicating this is a development or experimental implementation\n\nThe function is designed to be called as a console script entry point, providing a clean and minimal interface for launching the ACP server with proper initialization and error handling infrastructure in place.",
    "interfaces": [
      {
        "description": "Entry point function for the multi-session ACP server. Initializes logging and starts the asynchronous ACP server on stdio transport with experimental protocol support.",
        "interface_type": "function",

    "detailed_description": "This component serves as a type definition module for the Kimi CLI's ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) system. It defines union types that represent different variants of MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers and content blocks. The MCPServer type combines three different MCP server transport protocols (HTTP, SSE, and Stdio), while ACPContentBlock defines a union of five different content block types that can be used in ACP communications, including text, image, audio, and resource-based content blocks.",
